question: In SAP BTP, explain the relationship between a role template, a role, and a role collection.
hints: Think of the ABAP PFCG analogy but for XSUAA
Which one is what you actually assign to a user?
expectedAnswer: A role template is defined in an application's xs-security.json and ships with the app; a role is an instantiation of that template (optionally with attribute values filled in) inside a subaccount; a role collection bundles one or more roles from one or more applications and is the only object actually assigned to a user or group.
detailedAnswer: Role templates are declared by the application developer in xs-security.json and deployed into the subaccount when the app/service instance is bound - they are not directly assignable. In the BTP cockpit or via SCIM/APIs, an administrator creates roles from these templates, optionally filling in attribute-based restriction values. Role collections are the assignable unit: they aggregate roles across multiple applications (e.g., a business role spanning an SAP Analytics Cloud role and a custom Fiori app role) and are what gets mapped to users directly, to user groups, or via IdP group-to-role-collection assertion mapping. This three-layer model mirrors PFCG's object/role/composite pattern but is API- and JSON-driven rather than GUI-transaction-driven.
followUps: What happens to existing role assignments if a role template is removed from xs-security.json in a new app version?
Can a role collection contain roles from applications in different spaces?
commonMistakes: Trying to assign a role template or a bare role directly to a user instead of via a role collection
Assuming role collections are scoped to a single application
scenario: A new Fiori app's authorizations aren't showing up for any user despite the role template appearing correctly in xs-security.json.
diagram:
flowchart LR
  A[xs-security.json Role Template] --> B[Role Instance in Subaccount]
  B --> C[Role Collection]
  C --> D[User / Group Assignment]
  E[IdP Group Assertion] --> C

question: What is the difference between a scope and an attribute in XSUAA, and how do attributes enable instance-based (data-level) authorization?
hints: Scopes are binary yes/no; attributes carry values
expectedAnswer: A scope is a coarse-grained yes/no permission (e.g., 'can access this app/API') checked by the application at the endpoint level; an attribute is a named value (e.g., cost center, country) carried in the JWT that the application must explicitly evaluate itself to restrict data within that scope - XSUAA does not enforce attribute-based filtering automatically.
detailedAnswer: Scopes map roughly to authorization-object-level checks in ABAP terms - the app checks 'does this token contain scope X' before allowing an operation. Attributes are declared in xs-security.json under `attributes`, given values when a role is created from the template, and surfaced inside the JWT's `xs.system.attributes` (or custom attribute) claims. Critically, XSUAA itself does not filter any data based on attribute values - it is purely a carrier. The consuming application's business logic must read the attribute from the decoded token and apply the corresponding filter (e.g., WHERE cost_center IN (...)). This is a common architecture pitfall: teams assume attribute restriction is enforced automatically the way org-level fields are in derived ABAP roles, and ship an app that reads scopes but ignores attributes entirely, silently granting full data access to everyone with the scope.
followUps: How would you verify during a security review whether an application actually enforces attribute restrictions or silently ignores them?
How do attribute restrictions interact with role collections combining multiple roles with different attribute values for the same user?
commonMistakes: Assuming XSUAA enforces attribute-based data filtering automatically like ABAP org-level fields
Not testing whether a custom application actually reads and applies attribute claims from the JWT
scenario: A custom Fiori app grants the correct scope to a user but the user can still see cost center data outside their assigned attribute value.

question: Walk through what happens to a user's effective access when a role collection assignment is revoked while the user holds a still-valid JWT access token.
hints: JWT is a bearer token, not a session lookup
Think token lifetime vs revocation propagation
expectedAnswer: The revocation is not reflected until the current JWT expires and a new token is requested and issued, because XSUAA/OAuth access tokens are self-contained bearer tokens validated by signature and expiry alone - there is no per-request server-side check against current role collection assignments during the token's remaining lifetime.
detailedAnswer: When a role collection is unassigned in the BTP cockpit or via SCIM, that change updates the identity/authorization store immediately, but it does not invalidate tokens already issued. The access token (typically 12 hours default validity, configurable) carries the scopes that were valid at token issuance time baked into its claims, and resource servers validate the token purely via signature verification against the issuing XSUAA's public key plus expiry timestamp - no live authorization re-check occurs. This means a revoked user retains effective access for up to the remaining token lifetime, and if a long-lived refresh token was also issued, the user could silently obtain a fresh access token unless the refresh token is separately revoked. For high-risk terminations, this token-lifetime exposure window is a real and frequently underestimated architectural risk in BTP-based landscapes, distinct from on-premise ABAP where a locked user is rejected at the very next dialog logon.
followUps: How would you reduce this exposure window for privileged users without hurting UX for everyone?
What is the difference in exposure between revoking a role collection versus fully deactivating the user in the identity provider?
commonMistakes: Assuming a role collection revocation takes effect immediately like an ABAP role removal at next transaction call
Forgetting that refresh tokens must also be revoked, not just the access token's originating assignment
scenario: A terminated contractor's BTP role collection was removed immediately, but access logs show continued API activity for several hours afterward.
diagram:
sequenceDiagram
  participant U as User
  participant IdP as Identity Provider
  participant XSUAA
  participant App as Resource App
  U->>IdP: Authenticate
  IdP->>XSUAA: Assertion
  XSUAA->>U: Access Token (12h) + Refresh Token
  Note over XSUAA: Admin revokes Role Collection
  U->>App: API Call with old Access Token
  App->>App: Validate signature + expiry only
  App-->>U: Access still granted until token expiry

question: How would you design a multi-subaccount governance model for a BTP global account with 30+ subaccounts spanning dev, QA, and prod across multiple regions and business units?
hints: Think about what must be centrally enforced versus delegated per subaccount
Consider entitlement/quota management and drift detection
expectedAnswer: Centralize global account-level policy (entitlements, subaccount naming/structure, mandatory security defaults like trusted IdP configuration and password policy) while delegating day-to-day role collection assignment per subaccount to business-unit administrators, and implement automated drift detection comparing subaccount configuration against the approved baseline.
detailedAnswer: A 30+ subaccount landscape without central governance quickly produces inconsistent trust configuration (some subaccounts trusting the corporate IdP correctly, others left on the default identity provider), inconsistent entitlement allocation, and configuration drift nobody notices until an audit or incident. The architecture should define directories to logically group subaccounts by environment/business unit with directory-level policies inherited downward, enforce a mandatory baseline (default IdP trust, custom IdP disablement of self-registration, standard role collection naming) via Infrastructure-as-Code (e.g., BTP Terraform provider or Cloud Foundry/Kyma manifests plus the Cloud Management service APIs) rather than manual cockpit changes, and run a scheduled reconciliation job pulling actual subaccount configuration via API and diffing against the IaC-declared baseline to catch drift introduced by out-of-band manual changes. Entitlements and quotas should be managed centrally at the global account/directory level to prevent subaccount sprawl from silently exhausting shared capacity.
followUps: How would you retrofit this governance model onto 30 subaccounts that already have years of manual, undocumented configuration?
How do you handle a business unit that needs a genuinely different trust configuration than the corporate baseline?
commonMistakes: Managing subaccount configuration purely through the cockpit with no IaC or drift detection
Allowing every subaccount admin unrestricted authority to change trust/IdP configuration independently
scenario: An internal audit finds 6 of 30 BTP subaccounts still trust the default SAP identity provider instead of the corporate IdP, and nobody can explain why.

question: What is the difference between the XSUAA default identity zone trust and configuring a custom, corporate identity provider as a trust configuration in a subaccount?
hints: Think self-registration and password policy control
expectedAnswer: The default identity zone uses SAP ID Service with self-service registration and SAP-managed credentials, unsuitable for enterprise use; a custom trust configuration points the subaccount to a corporate IdP (typically via IAS as broker) so authentication, password policy, and MFA are governed centrally rather than by an SAP-managed consumer identity store.
detailedAnswer: Every new subaccount is pre-trusted to the SAP ID Service by default, which allows arbitrary self-registration of accounts using any email address - acceptable for a personal trial but a serious governance gap for enterprise production, since access reviews and joiner-mover-leaver processes have no visibility into or control over that identity store. Configuring a custom trust configuration (Security > Trust Configuration in the subaccount) establishes a SAML2/OIDC trust relationship to a corporate IdP, most commonly SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) itself acting as a broker to the true corporate IdP (Azure AD/Entra ID, Okta, etc.). Best practice is to disable or restrict the default identity provider's use for business applications entirely once the custom trust is established, forcing all interactive authentication through the governed corporate identity chain.
followUps: Why is disabling the default identity provider for production applications considered a hardening best practice?
How would you audit whether any application in a subaccount is still accidentally using the default IdP?
commonMistakes: Leaving the default identity provider active and reachable for production business applications
Assuming custom trust configuration alone removes the default IdP as an option without explicit restriction
scenario: A penetration test finds that a production Fiori app on BTP can still be accessed via self-registered SAP ID Service accounts.

question: A user reports they can access an API they should not have access to. You confirm their role collection assignment is correct and does not include the offending scope. What are the other places a scope could be coming from?
hints: Think group-to-role-collection mapping from the IdP assertion
Think inherited/nested role collections and multiple applications sharing scope names
expectedAnswer: Check IdP-side group assertions mapped to additional role collections the user may not realize they're a member of, check for a broader role collection assigned at a group level rather than individually, and check whether a different, unrelated application defines an identically-named scope that the resource server is mistakenly validating against.
detailedAnswer: Direct role collection-to-user assignment is only one path; users can also receive role collections transitively via IdP group assertions (SAML/OIDC group claims mapped to role collections in the trust configuration's 'Role Collection Mapping'), meaning the user's corporate IdP group membership - not anything visible in the BTP cockpit's direct user view - is granting the access. Second, check whether the user belongs to any BTP-managed group with its own role collection assignment. Third, and more subtle: scope names in XSUAA are only unique within an application's xs-security.json namespace by convention, but if a resource server's authorization check code is written loosely (checking for a scope string rather than the fully qualified `$XSAPPNAME.scopeName`), it could be satisfied by an identically-named scope granted via a completely different application's role collection. This last case is a real, hard-to-spot production bug pattern in multi-app landscapes.
followUps: How would you audit all IdP group-to-role-collection mappings across a subaccount?
Why is qualifying scope checks with the full xsappname critical in custom application code?
commonMistakes: Only checking direct user-to-role-collection assignment and stopping there
Not considering IdP group assertion mapping as an access source
scenario: A user with no direct role collection assignment for a finance API is still able to call it successfully.

question: How would you build a business case to justify moving from ad hoc, cockpit-driven BTP subaccount administration to a fully Infrastructure-as-Code managed model?
hints: Think audit findings, incident history, and onboarding time as quantifiable levers
expectedAnswer: Quantify the cost of the current state in terms of configuration drift incidents, audit findings related to undocumented manual changes, time to provision new subaccounts consistently, and disaster recovery risk, then present IaC adoption as directly reducing all of these with a phased rollout starting on new subaccounts before migrating existing ones.
detailedAnswer: Leadership will not fund a platform engineering exercise framed as technical hygiene alone. Build the case around measurable risk and cost: count how many audit findings or incidents in the past 12-24 months trace back to undocumented manual cockpit changes (drifted trust configuration, orphaned role collections, inconsistent entitlements), estimate current time-to-provision for a compliant new subaccount versus the target state with IaC templates, and frame disaster recovery - today, rebuilding a subaccount's configuration from scratch after a regional incident depends entirely on institutional memory and screenshots. Propose a phased adoption: mandate IaC for all newly created subaccounts immediately (low risk, no migration needed), then selectively migrate the highest-risk existing production subaccounts, deferring low-risk dev/sandbox subaccounts to last.
followUps: How would you handle resistance from admins comfortable with cockpit-based workflows?
What would you measure after 6 months to prove the investment paid off?
commonMistakes: Pitching IaC adoption purely as a best-practice/technical-elegance argument
Proposing a big-bang migration of all 30 existing subaccounts simultaneously
scenario: Leadership is skeptical of investing in Terraform/IaC tooling for BTP administration when the cockpit already 'works fine'.

question: Design a Zero Trust access model for a BTP landscape where business-critical APIs are consumed both by internal Fiori launchpad users and by external B2B partner systems.
hints: Think separate trust chains, separate token lifetimes/scopes per consumer type, and network-layer controls alongside identity
expectedAnswer: Segregate internal and partner access into distinct XSUAA-bound client configurations with different token lifetimes and scope grants, enforce mutual TLS or client-certificate-bound tokens for partner system-to-system calls in addition to OAuth scopes, and layer network/API-management-level controls (rate limiting, IP allowlisting via SAP API Management or equivalent) so no single credential compromise grants broad lateral access.
detailedAnswer: Zero Trust means no implicit trust based on network location alone, and every request must be authenticated and authorized on its own merits regardless of source. For internal Fiori users, standard OAuth authorization code flow through IAS-brokered corporate IdP with short-lived access tokens is appropriate. For B2B partner system-to-system calls, client credentials or certificate-bound (mTLS) OAuth tokens should be used instead of shared static API keys, issued through a distinct XSUAA service instance/client scoped only to the specific partner-facing scopes - never the same client used for internal users. Layer SAP API Management (or an equivalent API gateway) in front of both paths to enforce rate limiting, IP allowlisting for partner traffic, and centralized logging/anomaly detection independent of the application layer's own checks. Critically, avoid a single shared 'external API' role collection covering all partners; each partner relationship should map to its own scoped role collection so a single partner's credential compromise cannot be used to pivot to another partner's data, and access should be reviewed on the same cadence as internal privileged access.
followUps: How would you rotate partner credentials without causing an outage for that partner's integration?
How does this design change if a partner requires access to multiple subaccounts' APIs?
commonMistakes: Using the same XSUAA client and role collection design for internal users and external partners
Relying on network-layer controls (VPN, IP allowlist) alone without per-request identity-based authorization
scenario: A B2B partner integration was compromised and the incident review found the partner's credentials had the same scope breadth as internal Fiori users.

question: What is the difference between the XSUAA service plan 'application' and 'broker', and why does it matter for how role collections are structured?
hints: Think single dedicated app versus multi-tenant SaaS scenario
expectedAnswer: An 'application' plan XSUAA instance is bound to a single application for its own authentication/authorization needs; a 'broker' plan is used by multi-tenant SaaS applications to dynamically create dedicated XSUAA service instances per subscribing subaccount/tenant at subscription time, meaning role collections and trust are provisioned per tenant rather than shared.
detailedAnswer: Most custom BTP applications use a single 'application' plan XSUAA instance bound directly, with role templates and scopes defined once in that app's xs-security.json. Multi-tenant SaaS applications built for the SAP BTP marketplace or shared internally across subsidiaries instead use a 'broker' plan instance, which acts as a service broker: when a consumer subaccount subscribes to the SaaS app, the broker provisions a dedicated tenant-specific XSUAA configuration behind the scenes, including tenant-specific role collections that the consuming subaccount's administrators manage independently. Getting this wrong - e.g., using an 'application' plan for something that's actually meant to be consumed by many independent subaccounts - forces all tenants to share a single identity zone and role collection namespace, breaking tenant isolation.
followUps: How does tenant onboarding/offboarding differ operationally between these two plans?
What happens to a tenant's role collections if the SaaS application unsubscribes them?
commonMistakes: Using an 'application' plan for a genuinely multi-tenant SaaS scenario, breaking tenant isolation
Assuming broker-plan tenant provisioning happens for free without any onboarding automation work
scenario: A newly built multi-tenant SaaS application on BTP shows role collections from one customer subaccount visible to another.

question: How would you detect and remediate authorization drift across subaccounts that were originally cloned from the same template but have diverged over 18 months of independent administration?
hints: Think programmatic export/diff of role collections and trust configuration via API/CLI
Consider whether drift is always bad
expectedAnswer: Export the current role collection, role, and trust configuration state from every subaccount via the Cloud Management/XSUAA REST APIs or btp CLI, diff against the original template baseline (or against each other), and classify differences as either legitimate business-driven customization to retain or unauthorized drift to remediate - not all divergence is a defect.
detailedAnswer: Use the `btp` CLI or the Cloud Management and Authorization Management REST APIs to script an export of each subaccount's role collections (with their role and attribute assignments), trust configuration, and entitlements into a comparable format (JSON), then diff programmatically against the baseline template used at clone time, or pairwise across subaccounts if no baseline was preserved. Before remediating anything, classify each delta: some divergence is legitimate (a business unit genuinely needing an extra role collection for a local integration), while other divergence is unauthorized drift (a trust configuration change nobody documented, an overly broad role collection someone created to unblock a ticket and never cleaned up). Only the latter category should be remediated, and doing so requires stakeholder sign-off per subaccount since removing something assumed to be there breaks things quietly. Going forward, this comparison should become a scheduled automated job, not a one-time cleanup, feeding results into the governance model discussed for multi-subaccount management.
followUps: How would you avoid this drift accumulating again after the cleanup?
How do you handle a subaccount owner who disputes that their customization is unauthorized drift?
commonMistakes: Treating all configuration divergence as a defect to be forcibly reverted
Performing a one-time manual cleanup with no ongoing automated detection going forward
scenario: Eighteen months after cloning 8 subaccounts from a single approved template, security review finds each has meaningfully different role collections and trust settings.

question: What is the purpose of the xs-security.json file and what are its key sections?
hints: Deployed at app deploy time, not maintained via a UI
expectedAnswer: xs-security.json is the declarative security descriptor deployed alongside a BTP application defining its XSUAA service instance configuration - primarily its scopes, role templates, and attributes - which the platform uses to provision the app's authorization model at deploy time.
detailedAnswer: Unlike ABAP where authorization objects are checked in code and proposed via SU24, BTP applications declare their entire authorization surface upfront in xs-security.json: `scopes` (the permissions the app checks for), `attributes` (named values for instance-based restriction), `role-templates` (bundles of scopes and attribute placeholders that become assignable roles), and `oauth2-configuration` (redirect URIs, token validity). This file is passed to the XSUAA service during instance creation/binding (via `cf create-service` or mta.yaml), and any change requires a redeploy/instance update - there is no equivalent of live SU24 maintenance in production.
followUps: What happens if you change scopes in xs-security.json after roles have already been created from the old template?
How is xs-security.json typically managed in a CI/CD pipeline?
commonMistakes: Assuming xs-security.json changes take effect without redeploying/updating the service instance
Confusing role-templates in this file with the assignable role collections in the cockpit
scenario: A developer added a new scope to xs-security.json but it isn't appearing as an option when creating a new role in the subaccount.

question: Your team supports both S/4HANA on-premise authorization work and BTP authorization work. How do you structure the team and career development so consultants don't stagnate as pure ABAP-only or pure cloud-only specialists?
hints: Think rotation, paired work, and the long-term skills risk of a purely siloed team
expectedAnswer: Deliberately rotate consultants across both on-premise and BTP engagements rather than letting natural specialization calcify into permanent silos, pair less-experienced-in-cloud ABAP specialists with BTP-focused colleagues on hybrid projects, and set explicit growth expectations that senior team members must maintain working competence in both, since nearly every real landscape today is hybrid.
detailedAnswer: The market reality by 2025-2026 is that almost no serious SAP customer is purely on-premise or purely cloud - most run hybrid landscapes with S/4HANA plus BTP extensions, IAS/IPS identity federation, and SAC/Fiori cloud consumption, so a team siloed into 'ABAP people' and 'cloud people' will consistently produce architecture that's suboptimal at the seams between the two. Structure engagements to require pairing across the specialization boundary - a hybrid SSO or provisioning project naturally forces an ABAP security consultant to work alongside a BTP/IAS specialist. Set explicit expectations in career development conversations that senior consultants and leads are expected to build genuine working competence on both sides over time, not permanent specialization, and use internal training budget and certification support to back that expectation rather than leaving it as an unfunded mandate.
followUps: How would you handle a senior specialist who resists broadening beyond their comfort zone?
How do you measure whether this cross-training investment is actually improving project outcomes?
commonMistakes: Allowing permanent hard silos between 'ABAP security' and 'cloud security' team members
Treating cross-training as optional rather than an explicit expectation with resourcing behind it
scenario: A hybrid landscape project stalled repeatedly at the boundary between on-premise role design and BTP identity federation because no single consultant understood both sides well enough to own the integration.
